---
title: "Mapping the Numogram onto the Compson estate from 'The Sound and the Fury'"
output: html_document
---

Nick Land developed the “Numogram” or “Decimal Time-Circuit” in the 1990s through the Cybernetic Culture Research Unit at Warwick, a collective that emerged as a reaction to a growing digitization of culture. The Numogram is kind of their central artifact: a diagram of the decimal system which is treated as a hidden map of time and fate. 

### Numogram

You start with ten digits, 0 through 9, and use a process called digital reduction: repeatedly adding up a number's digits until only one remains. 29 becomes 11 (2 +9), which becomes 2 (1 +1). 347 becomes 14, which becomes 5. Apply this to every digit and a pattern emerges: the digits pair off by a kind of numerical symmetry. Each pairing that sums to N - 1 (9) is mapped. 1 and 8 sum to 9, as do 2 and 7, 3 and 6, and 4 and 5. The CCRU called these pairs syzygies, borrowing a term from astronomy where it describes the alignment of celestial bodies: a configuration of maximum gravitational tension.

Connect the digits by these syzygies, by the paths digital reduction traces between them, and you get the Numogram: a diagram with an internal structure, directional flows, and regions that behave differently from one another. It's a map in the way a circuit diagram organizes electrical relationships into something an engineer can reason about. The CCRU's bet was that it corresponded to real-life systems, especially systems of decay and transformation…exactly the kind of system the Compson estate turns out to be.

Here are the core pieces of the diagram that matter for what follows: 

- The Torque or “Time Circuit” (zones 1, 2, 4, 5, 7, 8) is the region governing closed, self-repeating cycles. Systems in the Torque try to preserve a fixed configuration against the pressure of time and instead of moving forward, they accumulate the past as a kind of debt. 
- The Warp (zones 3, 6) is the Torque's opposite: open, accelerative, indifferent to history: the flat, horizontal expanse of modern capital, what Deleuze and Guattari called deterritorialization (the process by which local, bounded structures get flattened and absorbed into larger, impersonal flows). The Warp has no memory nor loyalty, it simply replaces. 
- Gates are the specific vectors by which a point can exit one region and enter another. The whole structure is explicitly designed to think about how systems trap, channel, and occasionally release intensive flows: flows of energy, capital, trauma and time. They are one-directional and they do not preserve structure: passing through a Gate transforms the passing element into something that can no longer be described in the language of the zone it has left.
- Syzygies are the tension-pairings described above. They're the surfaces where the difference between two incompatible, bound-together zones gets converted into pressure. Think of two tectonic plates and the line between them of maximum stress where energy that can't go anywhere else builds up until it has to go somewhere.
- The Plex (zones 0, 9) is the ultimate, abyssal counterpart to both the Torque and the Warp: an immersive, oceanic depth rather than a flat, horizontal flow. Representing what the CCRU calls "Cthelll", the molten, metallic matrix of the planet, the Plex is the ungrounded, cosmic reservoir where linear time completely collapses into an absolute state of deep-past memory and non-local entanglement. It doesn't just accelerate or conserve; it acts as the primordial slime and the digital underworld from which all structures are born and back into which they must inevitably dissolve.

The diagram's basic unit is a flow… something must be moving, draining, returning, or crossing for any of this to do real work.

### Estate

The Sound and the Fury takes place almost entirely on or around the Compson property in Jefferson, Mississippi following the Civil War. It includes the house, the pasture which is sold off to become a golf course, the muddy creek (“the branch”) cutting through the grounds, the pear tree by the house, and the iron fence marking the property line. 

Think of the estate as a dynamical system: a set of possible states and a set of forces moving things between those states over time. The question this kind of system raises is about its long-run behavior: does it settle into stability? Loop in a stable pattern? Drift apart entirely? Read this way, the Compson estate has only one available long-run behavior… dissolution. There's no starting condition, no different set of choices, that produces a stable outcome for this family. The collapse is built into the shape of the property. It helps, too, to ask the question the Numogram always asks first: what is moving through this place? Where does it end up? Caddy moves through it, definitely. So does the money, doled out and drained and hoarded and stolen back. So do names: Maury, Quentin, Caddy itself…handed down, stripped off, and reused on a different person a generation later. So does Benjy’s particular repetitive scream (bellow) and discomfort, recurring at the fence, at Caddy's wedding, in the carriage going the wrong way around the Confederate monument in the center of town.

### House

The Compson house takes the family's current situation: grudges, inherited obligations, sense of pride/honor, arrangement of parlors and portraits, and feeds it back into itself, more or less unchanged. Call this the Torque. Each time it runs, what comes out the other end is the same configuration that went in: a system that doesn't expand into something new and doesn't contract toward resolution either, it repeats.

This is also what Quentin Compson and his mother are doing in their respective sections. Quentin carries the family's honor and obligations from Mississippi to Harvard, but the geographic move really changes nothing: his his fixation on time, purity/virginity, and Southern codes of conduct remain unchanged despite the new location. He spends his last day trying to help the young Italian immigrant girl find her way home around Cambridge, a total stranger he treats as a stand-in for Caddy who he failed to protect: the same controlling impulse replaying itself on someone who has nothing to do with his actual family. He even tries, at one point, to take Caddy's transgression onto himself, telling people he and Caddy had committed incest together…a lie meant to pull her sin back inside the family rather than leave it out in the world with Dalton Ames. It's an attempt to reverse an exit that has already happened, and it fails for the reason all such attempts fail: there's no operation inside the loop that undoes something that has already left it. He carries his grandfather's watch to Cambridge and breaks its crystal trying to symbolically stop time, and is left with a watch that still ticks, just without hands.

Mrs. Compson runs the same pattern at a slower frequency. Her renaming her son Maury to Benjy once she realizes that he's disabled so that her family name (Bascomb) remains idealized, her hypochondria, her constant invocation of Bascomb family pride, and her underlying preference for Jason as the only child she considers fully hers. None of this shifts from the beginning of the novel to the end, regardless of what happens around her. She and Quentin are the parts of the system the loop returns to the same configuration every time, because they're already moving in the loop's only available direction.

Even the family's names behave like currency inside this loop, circulating without ever leaving it. Benjy was born with the name Maury, after his mother's brother. Once his disability becomes undeniable, the name is pulled back out of him by his mother and returned to circulate safely among the Bascombs alone, as if a name could be quarantined from the person that carried it. The name Quentin runs the opposite route: given first to the dead grandfather, then to the son who drowns himself, then to the granddaughter the family treats as already disgraced before she's done anything to deserve it. The name accumulates debt as it passes hand to hand.

The house's architecture enforces the same pattern physically. Faulkner describes the house in literal decay: square and paintless, its portico rotting, sits on a rigid, patriarchal floor plan: a dark space on the wall where a mirror used to hang, rooms that place each family member into a fixed position…the architecture is the mechanism. Even the carriage rides reinforce it: when Luster is driving Benjy through town and takes the wrong way around the Confederate monument in the square, Benjy panics and screams uncontrollably until Jason intervenes and turns the carriage the correct way. The exact route matters more than almost anything else in Benjy’s world, because for a system like this one, deviation from the loop is itself unbearable. Meals happen, rooms are occupied, the rituals of the household continue. But money, land, social standing, and Caddy most of all is being subtracted with every cycle, even while the cycle itself looks undisturbed on the surface.

### Golf Course

If the house is a closed loop, the golf course is something stranger than its opposite. It's the point where an outside circuit (capital, leisure money, networks of ownership with no relationship to Jefferson, Mississippi whatsoever) crosses the property line and starts rewiring everything on the other side of it.

A bounded space (literally fenced and embedded in a local agrarian economy) has a defined edge beyond which it doesn't reach, and a history that belongs to just the people who worked/lived with it. That's what the Compson pasture was before it was sold. The golf course replacing it has no such edge: through developers and players and leisure money, it connects to networks extending far past the property line and never circling back. Call this the Warp.

The sale itself is the crucial event, and it's irreversible in a very specific way: there's no way to reconstruct, from inside the golf course, the local relationships (the particular history of labor and obligation tying that land to that family) the pasture used to embody. Once it's converted, the information needed to convert it back doesn't exist anymore. This is what the Compsons experience as ruin, and why their experience feels total and final.

The money from that sale does two things in the novel, and it's worth being specific here about where it goes: first, it literally pays for Quentin's year at Harvard and for Caddy's wedding to Herbert Head… meaning the very transaction that opens the closed pasture to the global market is the transaction funding the Torque's two most desperate, doomed attempts to hold itself together. The open system bankrolls the closed one's last failed efforts at self-preservation, which fail anyway, because money was never the actual problem.

Jason inherits the family's relationship to this drain and makes it worse. He speculates in cotton futures, tracking prices by telegraph from a New York broker, certain each time he's about to recoup what the family has lost. He intercepts the monthly support checks Caddy sends that are meant for her daughter, cashes the real ones for himself, and shows his mother forged checks he claims to be burning out of principle, while hoarding the actual cash in a locked box in his room. Jason is trying to fix a structural loss through transactions (speculation, theft, hoarding) none of which can work, because what's gone isn't money. It's really the local, bounded thing the money used to represent.

On the golf course itself, golfers calling for their caddie carries across the fence to where Benjy stands, and his mind, primed for one specific sound, hears his sister's name “Caddy” instead. The open system reaches across the boundary and triggers the closed one with a borrowed noise, using Benjy as the wire the signal travels along.

### Benjy and the Branch

It's tempting to describe Benjy's section as an extreme case of memory…as though 1898 were stored somewhere in him and certain triggers retrieve it. I believe that gets the mechanism backwards. Benjy doesn't move through time normally and occasionally fall back into the past when triggered. Time, for him, is produced locally, generated fresh at specific trigger points rather than retrieved from anywhere like a normal memory would be. A smell, a sound, a particular quality of cold air doesn't unlock some past stored experience. Instead, it builds a new one with exactly the same immediacy as whatever is actually happening on the calendar date the section is narrating. The two dates aren't separated in his experience because nothing is being separated and reunited. They're being manufactured on demand by whichever trigger fires.

The muddy creek running along the property (the branch) is where this production runs hottest, and it isn't just a poetic touch on Faulkner's part. Moving water mixes and swirls things around: points that start close together end up far apart, and points that start far apart end up next to each other. The branch doesn't store time so much as continuously generate it, stirring what was early and what was late into the same current and discharging whichever one a given trigger calls for. Call this abyssal, swirling matrix the Plex. 

A Cantor set is a strange object from mathematics, built by taking a line, removing its middle third, then removing the middle third of what's left, and repeating that forever. What remains afterward is kind of a dust: infinitely many isolated points with no continuous stretch connecting any of them, yet somehow still as numerous as the line you started with. 

Benjy's accessible past has this shape. Certain moments are preserved with total, almost unbearable clarity (the branch in 1898, the night their grandmother died and Caddy climbed the pear tree and got her drawers muddy looking in at the funeral through the window, Caddy's wedding day, the satsumas and cushion he was given as a small child, the single broken flower stalk he carries everywhere that Luster sometimes fixes and sometimes doesn't) while the vast stretches of ordinary time between these moments aren't merely forgotten. They were never there to begin with, in his experience, the way the removed sections of a Cantor set aren't damaged or erased, they simply never persisted as a continuous interval. Benjy associates Caddy, in the time before anything went wrong, with a particular clean smell; the moment that smell disappears, replaced by the perfume she starts wearing, registers in him as a wordless wrongness he has no way to explain, and he cries until she washes it off.

What remains is rich in detail but covers almost no continuous span of lived time, and that's exactly why reading his section produces the disorientation it does: a constant sense of almost locating yourself in time, followed by a sudden lurch to somewhere else entirely, triggered by something as small as a single word. When someone near the golf course fence calls out for a caddie, the jump from 1928 to 1898 happens instantly and completely, no gradual drift to some memory, just one state replaced by another. It's the correct way to represent a mind organized like this: a system where almost-identical starting points (two nearly identical sounds, two nearly identical smells) can lead to completely different temporal destinations, because nothing here is being recalled. It's being recomputed, fresh, every time the right trigger fires.
It helps to picture Benjy's memory as something like a strange attractor (the shape a chaotic system settles into over the long run, even though its moment-to-moment behavior stays unpredictable). The classic image is the Lorenz attractor, which is shaped like a butterfly. A trajectory spirals around one wing, jumps to the other, spirals there, jumps back, never repeating the same path exactly but never leaving the basic two-lobed shape either. 

*****
Benjy's memory has the same two-lobed structure: Caddy-present and Caddy-absent, the world before the loss and the world after it. Every thread of his experience eventually spirals back to one of these two poles. You can't predict, moment to moment, which one he'll land in next, but you can be certain he'll keep returning to one or the other, because the shape of his memory doesn't allow for a third option.

This also means Benjy doesn't experience time as having a direction… as being linear. The distinction between before and after, which organizes almost everyone else's sense of their own life, doesn't organize his. Any given moment in his story is as central, as present, as any other. Proximity to Caddy matters more than position on a calendar. There's something almost like the mystic's eternal present in this: a state where the difference between was and is stops doing any work. The cruelty is exactly that this eternal present is an eternal present of loss. Both lobes of his attractor have Caddy in them: one with her, one without, and his mind can never settle on the first one permanently. It can only keep returning. In this way, Benjy exists outside of linear time. 

### Pear Tree

The pear tree is the Gate. It is the only piece of this system that allows a real exit. It places people from inside the house into the wider world, but it doesn't transport them with their old identity intact. Whatever passes through undergoes a radical decoding, coming out as something the house's own vocabulary can no longer describe. 

This is the structural reason Caddy can't be narrated directly anywhere in the novel. In 1959 speaking at the University of Virginia, Faulkner said the book began for him with a single image: Caddy in the pear tree, looking through a window at her grandmother's funeral, her muddy drawers visible to her brothers watching from below. The four sections that follow are four attempts, by four different narrators, to capture a person who has already left the space those narrators occupy. Her marriage to Herbert Head is itself an attempted re-closure: an effort to bring her back inside a respectable, contained arrangement. It fails almost immediately: Herbert discovers she's pregnant by Dalton Ames before the marriage and divorces her, and she's banished from the family for good. The patch doesn't work because what's being patched is a structural problem not a social one. Once she's through the Gate, no operation available inside the Torque can bring her back the way she was.

Quentin's suicide is the mirror image of this. He doesn't pass through any gate, there isn't one available to him the way there is for Caddy. Instead of exiting the loop, he ends his own trajectory inside it, weighting his clothes with flatirons and walking into the Charles River. Where Caddy's exit transforms her into something the house can't describe, Quentin's failure to find an exit just stops the loop from his end entirely.
A generation later, Caddy's daughter (also named Quentin, after her uncle) performs the same operation her mother did, climbing down from a window using that same pear tree to run off with a man from a traveling show, taking with her the cash Jason has been hoarding from her stolen support checks. This is a recurrence in the way that the CCRU cared about: the system doesn't generate a new kind of event here, it rediscovers a configuration it already contains, running the same exit through the same tree on a different person, a different decade later. The money drained out of the open system and hoarded inside the closed one finds its own gate and leaves through it too: the same operation, performed on cash instead of a person, releasing it back into the flat flows of the Warp. What this shows is that the Gate is a permanent feature, and the estate doesn't so much produce events as produce recurrences of a small number of available configurations. The fence tries to hold. The tree offers a way out. Both are always there, for every generation (and for every dollar) that passes through this property.

### Fence

The iron fence is something between being a literal wall separating the house from the golf course and a symbol of the tension between them. it's a kind of relay: a specific point where a signal generated in one regime crosses into the other and gets perceived as something completely different.
A golfer's shout for his caddie is generated inside the Warp's economy of leisure and service (the golf course); it crosses the fence and arrives, on the Compson side as the sound of the name “Caddy.” The fence's job is to carry traffic across that gap, badly, with the signal arriving scrambled every time. That makes the fence one syzygy among several the estate produces. 

Quentin and Jason are also a syzygy in their own right: two responses to the exact same inherited debt, routed in opposite directions. Quentin converts the family's stockpiled honor into pure negation. Time itself becomes his enemy, and the only available exit is to stop being a system that can be acted on at all. Jason, on the other hand, converts the identical inheritance he received into exchange. Every loss becomes a transaction he might still win, every grievance a number he's chasing on a ticker. Between them they exhaust the loop's two available outputs, total withdrawal and total commodification, and neither one actually settles the family's account.

Benjy and Caddy are a syzygy of a different kind: presence and absence paired so tightly that one can't be described without the other. Benjy doesn't miss Caddy the way a person misses a memory. He's connected to her directly, and her absence registers as a specific, located ache that has to be discharged through something physical: pacing the fence, bellowing at the gate, reacting to that borrowed sound carried over from the other side. He's the syzygy turned into a person, his is what the system uses to keep the differential between Caddy-present and Caddy-absent from simply dissipating.

Benjy's daily routine of pacing the fence puts him exactly at this point of maximum sensitivity. The spot where the smallest disturbance produces the largest response. A golfer's shout, a change in the weather, the wrong word from Luster: any of these can trigger a complete reorientation, because he's standing precisely where the estate's strongest signals all cross at once.

His castration, which is spoken about retrospectively and is a reaction to a frightening, ambiguous incident at the gate involving a schoolgirl walking home, is the estate inscribing that differential directly onto his body. He's marked, permanently, as the place where presence and absence, the house's closed world and the golf course's open one, all fail to reconcile at once. His bellowing (at the fence, at his sister's wedding, in the carriage going the wrong way around the square) is the closest thing this system has to a readout: the sound a living person makes standing at the point where several incompatible signals are arriving simultaneously, with no other way to register it.

Faulkner says he spent years trying to get this novel right and said, long after publishing it, that he never fully succeeded, that of everything he'd written, this was the one he most wished he could go back and fix. Read through the Numogram, the failure looks necessary rather than accidental: Caddy can't be captured because she's already passed through the Gate, and nothing that has passed through a Gate can be described from inside the space it left. The novel's inability to narrate its own center is actually the correct output of the system the writing describes.

The estate runs. The trajectory converges. The dissolution completes itself.
